function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The Space segment absorbed $3,004M of Starship R&D in FY2025 against $4,086M of segment revenue. If that Starship R&D were treated as an unallocated corporate expense instead of segment expense, what would Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA be? What does this indicate about segment-level profitability presentation?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's S-1 filing. I need to find information about:
1. Space segment Starship R&D of $3,004M in FY2025
2. Space segment revenue of $4,086M in FY2025
3. Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA

Let me retrieve information from the SpaceX S-1 filing.

arguments.prompt: What is the Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}? Also retrieve the Space segment revenue, Starship R&D expense of $3,004M, and any segment reconciliation tables showing how Adjusted EBITDA is calculated.

Find the segment financial information table in {{spacex_s1}} showing Space segment revenue, cost of revenue, R&D expenses, and Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025. Include any reconciliation of segment Adjusted EBITDA.

arguments.prompt: What is the Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}? Also retrieve the Space segment revenue, Starship R&D expense of $3,004M, and any segment reconciliation tables showing how Adjusted EBITDA is calculated.

Find the segment financial information table in {{spacex_s1}} showing Space segment revenue, cost of revenue, R&D expenses, and Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025. Include any reconciliation of segment Adjusted EBITDA.

results.content:
# Space Segment FY2025 Financial Summary

## Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025: **$653 million**

This represents a decrease of $501 million compared to $1,154 million in 2024, primarily driven by accelerated investment in Starship vehicle development and launch facilities, partially offset by increased NASA Cargo Resupply Services (CRS) missions and revenue from a U.S. Department of War contract.

## Additional Requested Metrics

| Metric | FY2025 Value |
|--------|-------------|
| Space Segment Revenue | $4,086 million |
| Space Segment Loss from Operations | $(657) million |
| Starship R&D Expense | $3,004 million |
| Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $653 million |

## Segment Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation Table (Year Ended December 31, 2025)

| Line Item (in millions) | Space |
|------------------------|-------|
| Income (loss) from operations | $(657) |
| Add: Depreciation and amortization | 757 |
| Add: Share-based compensation | 515 |
| Add: Restructuring charges | — |
| Add: Impairment | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** |

## Key Context

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is defined as segment income (loss) from operations excluding:
1. Depreciation and amortization
2. Share-based compensation
3. Restructuring charges
4. Impairment

The Space segment became Segment Adjusted EBITDA positive on a sustained basis beginning in 2018. The $3,004 million in Starship R&D expense was a significant driver of the year-over-year decline in Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA, as the company accelerated investment in the next-generation Starship launch vehicle program designed to enable step-function improvements in reusability, payload capacity, and launch cadence.

Based on the provided text, here is the segment financial information table for the year ended December 31, 2025, detailing the Space segment's revenue, cost of revenue, R&D expenses, and other financial metrics, along with the reconciliation of segment income (loss) from operations to Segment Adjusted EBITDA. ### Segment Financial Information (Year Ended December 31, 2025) *(in millions)* | | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total Reportable Segments | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Revenue** | $4,086 | $11,387 | $3,201 | $18,674 | | **Costs and expenses** | | | | | | Cost of revenue | 1,352 | 5,921 | 2,178 | 9,451 | | Research and development | 3,004 | 575 | 5,064 | 8,643 | | Selling, general, and administrative | 349 | 468 | 1,827 | 2,644 | | Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 | | Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 | | **Total costs and expenses** | 4,743 | 6,964 | 9,556 | 21,263 | | **Income (loss) from operations** | (657) | 4,423 | (6,355) | (2,589) | | Interest expense | | | | (1,945) | | Interest income | | | | 492 | | Other income (expense), net | | | | (177) | | **Income (loss) before income taxes** | | | | $(4,219) | | **Supplemental segment information** | | | | | | Depreciation and amortization | $757 | $2,376 | $3,568 | $6,701 | | Share-based compensation | $515 | $369 | $1,063 | $1,947 | | Impairment | $38 | $— | $— | $38 | | Capital expenditures | $3,832 | $4,178 | $12,727 | $20,737 |
### Reconciliation of Segment Income (Loss) from Operations to Segment Adjusted EBITDA *(Year Ended December 31, 2025 - in millions)* | | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total Reportable Segments | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Income (loss) from operations** | $(657) | $4,423 | $(6,355) | $(2,589) | | **Add:** | | | | | | Depreciation and amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 | | Share-based compensation | 515 | 369 | 1,063 | 1,947 | | Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 | | Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 | | **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$7,168** | **$(1,237)** | **$6,584** |

steps.message: I have the key data I need. Let me verify the calculations.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the key information I need:

Space Segment FY2025:
- Revenue: $4,086M
- Cost of revenue: $1,352M
- R&D: $3,004M (this is the Starship R&D)
- SG&A: $349M
- Restructuring: $0
- Impairment: $38M
- Total costs and expenses: $4,743M
- Loss from operations: $(657)M
- D&A: $757M
- SBC: $515M
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $653M

The question asks: If the $3,004M of Starship R&D were treated as an unallocated corporate expense instead of segment expense, what would Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA be?

So we need to add back the $3,004M of Starship R&D to the Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

Current Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA = $653M
If we add back $3,004M of Starship R&D:
$653M + $3,004M = $3,657M

Let me verify this calculation:
- Loss from operations: $(657)M
- Add back Starship R&D: $3,004M
- New income from operations: $(657) + $3,004 = $2,347M
- Add D&A: $757M
- Add SBC: $515M
- Add Impairment: $38M
- New Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $2,347 + $757 + $515 + $38 = $3,657M

Yes, $3,657M.

Let me also calculate the margin:
- Current margin: $653M / $4,086M = 15.98%
- New margin: $3,657M / $4,086M = 89.50%

This indicates that the segment-level profitability presentation is highly sensitive to how Starship R&D is allocated. The Space segment's reported Adjusted EBITDA of $653M (a 16% margin) would jump to $3,657M (an 89.5% margin) if Starship R&D were treated as unallocated corporate expense rather than segment expense.

This is a significant observation about segment-level profitability presentation:
1. The Space segment's profitability is heavily influenced by the allocation of Starship R&D
2. Starship R&D represents ~73% of Space segment revenue ($3,004M / $4,086M)
3. The current presentation shows Space as marginally profitable on an Adjusted EBITDA basis
4. If Starship R&D were treated as corporate/unallocated, Space would appear dramatically more profitable
5. This raises questions about whether Starship R&D should be considered a segment cost or a strategic corporate investment
6. The choice of allocation methodology significantly impacts how investors perceive segment-level profitability

Let me also calculate the percentage of revenue that Starship R&D represents:
$3,004M / $4,086M = 73.5%

And the percentage of total costs and expenses:
$3,004M / $4,743M = 63.3%

Let me also verify the calculation once more:
- Current Adjusted EBITDA: $653M
- Add back Starship R&D: $3,004M
- New Adjusted EBITDA: $653M + $3,004M = $3,657M

Margin:
- Current: $653M / $4,086M = 15.98%
- New: $3,657M / $4,086M = 89.50%

Let me use the calculator to verify these calculations.

steps.message: I have all the information needed to answer the question. Let me submit the final result.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the calculations I need. Let me prepare the final answer.

Key findings:
1. Current Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA (FY2025): $653M
2. If Starship R&D ($3,004M) is treated as unallocated corporate expense: $653M + $3,004M = $3,657M
3. Current Adjusted EBITDA margin: 15.98%
4. New Adjusted EBITDA margin: 89.50%
5. Starship R&D as % of revenue: 73.52%
6. Starship R&D as % of total costs and expenses: 63.34%

This indicates that the segment-level profitability presentation is highly sensitive to the allocation of Starship R&D. The Space segment's reported Adjusted EBITDA of $653M (16% margin) would jump to $3,657M (89.5% margin) if Starship R&D were treated as unallocated corporate expense.

Let me also note that the Space segment's loss from operations is $(657)M, but Adjusted EBITDA is positive $653M because of the add-backs (D&A, SBC, impairment). The Starship R&D is the dominant cost driver.

Let me also verify the reconciliation:
- Loss from operations: $(657)M
- Add D&A: $757M
- Add SBC: $515M
- Add Impairment: $38M
- = $653M ✓

If we add back Starship R&D:
- Loss from operations: $(657)M + $3,004M = $2,347M
- Add D&A: $757M
- Add SBC: $515M
- Add Impairment: $38M
- = $3,657M ✓

Now let me prepare the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Answer

### Recasting Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA Excluding Starship R&D

**Reported Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA (FY2025): $653M**

If the $3,004M of Starship R&D were reclassified from a segment expense to an unallocated corporate expense, Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA would be:

**$653M + $3,004M = $3,657M**

### Reconciliation (in $ millions)

| Line Item | As Reported | Starship R&D Reclassified to Corporate |
|---|---:|---:|
| Revenue | $4,086 | $4,086 |
| Cost of revenue | (1,352) | (1,352) |
| Research and development | (3,004) | — |
| SG&A | (349) | (349) |
| Impairment | (38) | (38) |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | **(657)** | **2,347** |
| Add: D&A | 757 | 757 |
| Add: Share-based compensation | 515 | 515 |
| Add: Impairment | 38 | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$3,657** |
| Adjusted EBITDA margin | 15.98% | 89.50% |

### What This Indicates About Segment-Level Profitability Presentation

1. **Extreme sensitivity to allocation methodology.** A single allocation decision — whether Starship R&D sits inside the Space segment or in unallocated corporate — swings Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA by roughly **5.6x** (from $653M to $3,657M) and the segment margin from ~16% to ~90%. The reported segment profitability is therefore not a robust indicator of the underlying economics of the Space business; it is heavily dependent on management's classification choice.

2. **Starship R&D dominates the segment cost structure.** Starship R&D of $3,004M equals **73.5% of Space segment revenue** and **63.3% of total Space segment costs and expenses**. The segment's reported operating loss of $(657)M is essentially a Starship R&D story — without it, the segment would have generated $2,347M of operating income on $4,086M of revenue.

3. **Adjusted EBITDA masks the operating loss.** The reported $653M of positive Adjusted EBITDA is only achievable because D&A ($757M), SBC ($515M), and impairment ($38M) add-backs more than offset the $(657)M GAAP operating loss. Stripping out Starship R&D as well produces a dramatically different picture, illustrating that the non-GAAP measure is highly sensitive to which costs are allocated to the segment versus excluded via add-backs.

4. **Strategic-investment framing.** Starship is a next-generation platform whose development benefits the entire enterprise (potentially the Connectivity/Starlink and AI segments as well, via launch capacity and cadence). Treating it as a segment cost makes the Space segment look marginally profitable; treating it as a corporate strategic investment would make Space look like a high-margin cash generator. The choice materially affects how investors assess segment-level unit economics and capital allocation.

5. **Disclosure implication.** Because the recast consolidated financials include xAI and X Holdings under common-control accounting, and because Starship R&D is the single largest line item in the Space segment, investors should view Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a presentation-dependent metric rather than a clean read on the segment's standalone profitability. The filing's allocation of Starship R&D to the Space segment is a conservative choice that depresses reported segment profitability relative to an unallocated-corporate treatment.

### Sources

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Segment Financial Information and Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Answer

### Recasting Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA Excluding Starship R&D

**Reported Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA (FY2025): $653M**

If the $3,004M of Starship R&D were reclassified from a segment expense to an unallocated corporate expense, Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA would be:

**$653M + $3,004M = $3,657M**

### Reconciliation (in $ millions)

| Line Item | As Reported | Starship R&D Reclassified to Corporate |
|---|---:|---:|
| Revenue | $4,086 | $4,086 |
| Cost of revenue | (1,352) | (1,352) |
| Research and development | (3,004) | — |
| SG&A | (349) | (349) |
| Impairment | (38) | (38) |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | **(657)** | **2,347** |
| Add: D&A | 757 | 757 |
| Add: Share-based compensation | 515 | 515 |
| Add: Impairment | 38 | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$3,657** |
| Adjusted EBITDA margin | 15.98% | 89.50% |

### What This Indicates About Segment-Level Profitability Presentation

1. **Extreme sensitivity to allocation methodology.** A single allocation decision — whether Starship R&D sits inside the Space segment or in unallocated corporate — swings Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA by roughly **5.6x** (from $653M to $3,657M) and the segment margin from ~16% to ~90%. The reported segment profitability is therefore not a robust indicator of the underlying economics of the Space business; it is heavily dependent on management's classification choice.

2. **Starship R&D dominates the segment cost structure.** Starship R&D of $3,004M equals **73.5% of Space segment revenue** and **63.3% of total Space segment costs and expenses**. The segment's reported operating loss of $(657)M is essentially a Starship R&D story — without it, the segment would have generated $2,347M of operating income on $4,086M of revenue.

3. **Adjusted EBITDA masks the operating loss.** The reported $653M of positive Adjusted EBITDA is only achievable because D&A ($757M), SBC ($515M), and impairment ($38M) add-backs more than offset the $(657)M GAAP operating loss. Stripping out Starship R&D as well produces a dramatically different picture, illustrating that the non-GAAP measure is highly sensitive to which costs are allocated to the segment versus excluded via add-backs.

4. **Strategic-investment framing.** Starship is a next-generation platform whose development benefits the entire enterprise (potentially the Connectivity/Starlink and AI segments as well, via launch capacity and cadence). Treating it as a segment cost makes the Space segment look marginally profitable; treating it as a corporate strategic investment would make Space look like a high-margin cash generator. The choice materially affects how investors assess segment-level unit economics and capital allocation.

5. **Disclosure implication.** Because the recast consolidated financials include xAI and X Holdings under common-control accounting, and because Starship R&D is the single largest line item in the Space segment, investors should view Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a presentation-dependent metric rather than a clean read on the segment's standalone profitability. The filing's allocation of Starship R&D to the Space segment is a conservative choice that depresses reported segment profitability relative to an unallocated-corporate treatment.

    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Segment Financial Information and Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation"
    }
  ]
}
